key_insight: The paper defines a Hausdorff-distance framework on solution sets to quantify when an ODE can be uniquely and stably recovered from observed trajectories.
engineering_relevance: This gives a theoretical sample-complexity guide for scientific machine learning systems that infer governing equations from experimental or simulation data.
method: Treat each candidate ODE as a set of possible solutions over admissible initial conditions.
Measure equation mismatch using Hausdorff distance between solution sets to capture worst-case distinguishability.
Derive identifiability bounds and metric-entropy estimates for linear and Lipschitz/Hölder nonlinear ODE classes.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical identifiability and metric-entropy/sample-complexity results across multiple ODE classes, rather than an empirical benchmark.
watch_item: The guarantees are theory-driven and depend on assumptions such as the admissible solution set, regularity of the vector field, and availability of multiple informative trajectories.
paper_title: Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs
